All his three brothers, including Mikeiri, supported his decision to embark on the military campaign to eastern Japan.<ref>''Nihongi'' (W.G. Aston tr., Tuttle 1972 reprint), p. 111</ref><ref>http://www.louis-chor.ca/Nihon-Shoki-Trilingual-January-2020-1.pdf Selections from Nihon-Shoki, ch. 3</ref> Later during the military campaign, Mikeiri disappeared at sea<ref name="nihongi-1972a" /><ref name="mw-2" /> alongside [[:en:Inahi no Mikoto|Inahi no Mikoto]] and as a result, Jimmu was left the sole leader and became Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamuyamatoiwarebiko |url=https://archive.today/20230930215114/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9379 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>
=== Takachiho myth === However, [[:en:Takachiho, Miyazaki|Takachiho]] legend offers a different account, suggesting he returned to [[:en:Takachiho, Miyazaki|Takachiho]] to govern it.<ref name="the-official-miyazaki-prefecture-travel-guide-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Main Sanctuary, Takachiho Shrine |url=https://visitmiyazaki.com/mythology/main-sanctuary/ |access-date=2023-09-30 |website=The Official Miyazaki Prefecture Travel Guide |language=en-US}}</ref> The legend further narrates that a formidable kami deity named Kihachi, residing in a cave on Mt. Futagami, often terrorized the residents of Araragi Village. The climax of his reign of terror came when he kidnapped Princess Unome, intending to forcibly marry her. Mikeiri no Mikoto, upon his return to Takachiho, saw a haunting reflection of Princess Unome in a pond by the Gokase River. This vision revealed Kihachi's sinister intentions. In response, Mikeiri rallied a group of forty-four vassals to storm Kihachi's cave and rescue the princess.<ref name="mw-2" /><ref name="the-official-miyazaki-prefecture-travel-guide-2023a" />
Despite the initial victory, Kihachi was not easily subdued. After his defeat, he resurrected the very next day, burning with rage. In their subsequent encounter, Mikeiri took drastic measures by severing Kihachi into three distinct sections: head, torso, and limbs. Each segment was buried separately to ensure Kihachi's permanent incapacitation. Following this victory, Mikeiri and Princess Unome married, eventually having eight children. The legend asserts that their descendants later ruled over the region of Takachiho.<ref name="mw-2" />
== Modern worship == Today, physical reminders of this legend are scattered throughout [[:en:Takachiho, Miyazaki|Takachiho]]. Among them are the Nanatsuga Pond, where legend says Mikeiri first encountered Princess Unome's reflection, and the Chikara Ishi, a massive boulder believed to have been thrown by Kihachi during their initial battle. Distinct markers indicate the burial spots of Kihachi's divided body: his head is near the Solest Takachiho Hotel, the torso is proximate to Ryokan Shinsen, and his limbs are found near the ruins of Awaji Castle, adjacent to Takachiho High School.<ref name="mw-2" />
In modern [[:en:Takachiho, Miyazaki|Takachiho]], local folklore says that that Kihachi's vengeful spirit occasionally stirs, casting early frosts that harm the crops. To counteract this, the [[Takachiho Shrine]] hosts an annual event known as the Shishikake Festival. The ceremony involves offering a boar and the chanting of special prayers by Shinto priests to appease Kihachi's spirit. Additionally, Kagura, a traditional Shinto dance, is showcased during the festival, typically taking place on the third day of the twelfth lunar month, often coinciding with January.<ref name="mw-2" /> ==Family tree== {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== See also ==

{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | father = [[Ugayafukiaezu]] | mother = [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] | spouse = Princess Unome | siblings = Emperor Jimmu }} '''Mikeiri no Mikoto''' ([[:en:Japanese language|Japanese]]: 三毛入野命, also known as Mikenu, d. 663 BC according to traditional dating) is a Japanese deity. He was the older brother of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].<ref name="mw-2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001560306.pdf|title=Legend Mikeiri no Mikoto and Kihachi}}</ref> While playing a minor role in the [[Nihon Shoki]],<ref name="nihongi-1972a">''Nihongi'' (W.G. Aston tr., Tuttle 1972 reprint), p. 114</ref><ref name="mw-2" /> he has a prominent local tradition at [[Takachiho Shrine]]<ref name="mw-2" /> of which he is the main deity.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Click the english button to switch the page language to english since that is the page I am citing, not the Japanese version |url=https://www.miyazaki-archive.jp/d-museum/mch/kagura/nokatano-movie.html |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.miyazaki-archive.jp}}</ref> There is an annual festival for him called Shishikake Festival.<ref name="mw-2" />
== Myths ==
=== Nihon Shoki === According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], Mikeiri no Mikoto, [[Ugayafukiaezu]]'s third son and the older brother of Japan's first Emperor Jimmu, assisted his brother the Emperor in government affairs. All his three brothers, including Mikeiri, supported his decision to embark on the military campaign to eastern Japan.<ref>''Nihongi'' (W.G. Aston tr., Tuttle 1972 reprint), p. 111</ref><ref>http://www.louis-chor.ca/Nihon-Shoki-Trilingual-January-2020-1.pdf Selections from Nihon-Shoki, ch. 3</ref> Later during the military campaign, Mikeiri disappeared at sea<ref name="nihongi-1972a" /><ref name="mw-2" /> alongside [[:en:Inahi no Mikoto|Inahi no Mikoto]] and as a result, Jimmu was left the sole leader and became Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamuyamatoiwarebiko |url=https://archive.today/20230930215114/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9379 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>
=== Takachiho myth === However, [[:en:Takachiho, Miyazaki|Takachiho]] legend offers a different account, suggesting he returned to [[:en:Takachiho, Miyazaki|Takachiho]] to govern it.<ref name="the-official-miyazaki-prefecture-travel-guide-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Main Sanctuary, Takachiho Shrine |url=https://visitmiyazaki.com/mythology/main-sanctuary/ |access-date=2023-09-30 |website=The Official Miyazaki Prefecture Travel Guide |language=en-US}}</ref> The legend further narrates that a formidable kami deity named Kihachi, residing in a cave on Mt. Futagami, often terrorized the residents of Araragi Village. The climax of his reign of terror came when he kidnapped Princess Unome, intending to forcibly marry her. Mikeiri no Mikoto, upon his return to Takachiho, saw a haunting reflection of Princess Unome in a pond by the Gokase River. This vision revealed Kihachi's sinister intentions. In response, Mikeiri rallied a group of forty-four vassals to storm Kihachi's cave and rescue the princess.<ref name="mw-2" /><ref name="the-official-miyazaki-prefecture-travel-guide-2023a" />
Despite the initial victory, Kihachi was not easily subdued. After his defeat, he resurrected the very next day, burning with rage. In their subsequent encounter, Mikeiri took drastic measures by severing Kihachi into three distinct sections: head, torso, and limbs. Each segment was buried separately to ensure Kihachi's permanent incapacitation. Following this victory, Mikeiri and Princess Unome married, eventually having eight children. The legend asserts that their descendants later ruled over the region of Takachiho.<ref name="mw-2" />
== Modern worship == Today, physical reminders of this legend are scattered throughout [[:en:Takachiho, Miyazaki|Takachiho]]. Among them are the Nanatsuga Pond, where legend says Mikeiri first encountered Princess Unome's reflection, and the Chikara Ishi, a massive boulder believed to have been thrown by Kihachi during their initial battle. Distinct markers indicate the burial spots of Kihachi's divided body: his head is near the Solest Takachiho Hotel, the torso is proximate to Ryokan Shinsen, and his limbs are found near the ruins of Awaji Castle, adjacent to Takachiho High School.<ref name="mw-2" />
In modern [[:en:Takachiho, Miyazaki|Takachiho]], local folklore says that that Kihachi's vengeful spirit occasionally stirs, casting early frosts that harm the crops. To counteract this, the [[Takachiho Shrine]] hosts an annual event known as the Shishikake Festival. The ceremony involves offering a boar and the chanting of special prayers by Shinto priests to appease Kihachi's spirit. Additionally, Kagura, a traditional Shinto dance, is showcased during the festival, typically taking place on the third day of the twelfth lunar month, often coinciding with January.<ref name="mw-2" /> ==Family tree== {{Three generations of Hyuga}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Mikeiri no Mikoto]][[ja:三毛入野命]][[ko:미케누노미코토]][[zh:三毛野命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Mikeiri no Mikoto]][[ja:三毛入野命]][[ko:미케누노미코토]][[zh:三毛野命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Mikeiri no Mikoto]][[ja:三毛入野命]][[ko:미케누노미코토]][[zh:三毛野命]] {{Infobox person|death_place={{ill|Kumano Sea|WD=Q11568930}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|father={{ill|Ugayafukiaezu|WD=Q1373935}}|mother={{ill|Tamayori-bime|WD=Q3082751}}|relatives={{ill|Hiko Itsuse no Mikoto|WD=Q10881970}}; {{ill|Inai no Mikoto|WD=Q11420805}}; {{ill|Jimmu|WD=Q200188}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11071195]] Wikidata name: Mikeiri no Mikoto [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Mythical brother of Japan's first Emperor}}
{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | father = [[Ugayafukiaezu]] | mother = [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] | spouse = Princess Unome | siblings = Emperor Jimmu }} '''Mikeiri no Mikoto''' ({{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|WD=Q5287|lt=Japanese}}: 三毛入野命, also known as Mikenu, d. 663 BC according to traditional dating) is a Japanese deity. He was the older brother of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}.<ref name="mw-2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001560306.pdf|title=Legend Mikeiri no Mikoto and Kihachi}}</ref> While playing a minor role in the [[Nihon Shoki]],<ref name="nihongi-1972a">''Nihongi'' (W.G. Aston tr., Tuttle 1972 reprint), p. 114</ref><ref name="mw-2" /> he has a prominent local tradition at [[Takachiho Shrine]]<ref name="mw-2" /> of which he is the main deity.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Click the english button to switch the page language to english since that is the page I am citing, not the Japanese version |url=https://www.miyazaki-archive.jp/d-museum/mch/kagura/nokatano-movie.html |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.miyazaki-archive.jp}}</ref> There is an annual festival for him called Shishikake Festival.<ref name="mw-2" />
== Myths ==
=== Nihon Shoki === According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], Mikeiri no Mikoto, [[Ugayafukiaezu]]'s third son and the older brother of Japan's first Emperor Jimmu, assisted his brother the Emperor in government affairs. All his three brothers, including Mikeiri, supported his decision to embark on the military campaign to eastern Japan.<ref>''Nihongi'' (W.G. Aston tr., Tuttle 1972 reprint), p. 111</ref><ref>http://www.louis-chor.ca/Nihon-Shoki-Trilingual-January-2020-1.pdf Selections from Nihon-Shoki, ch. 3</ref> Later during the military campaign, Mikeiri disappeared at sea<ref name="nihongi-1972a" /><ref name="mw-2" /> alongside {{ill|Inahi no Mikoto|en|Inahi no Mikoto|WD=Q11420805|lt=Inahi no Mikoto}} and as a result, Jimmu was left the sole leader and became Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamuyamatoiwarebiko |url=https://archive.today/20230930215114/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9379 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>
=== Takachiho myth === However, {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|WD=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho}} legend offers a different account, suggesting he returned to {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|WD=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho}} to govern it.<ref name="the-official-miyazaki-prefecture-travel-guide-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Main Sanctuary, Takachiho Shrine |url=https://visitmiyazaki.com/mythology/main-sanctuary/ |access-date=2023-09-30 |website=The Official Miyazaki Prefecture Travel Guide |language=en-US}}</ref> The legend further narrates that a formidable kami deity named Kihachi, residing in a cave on Mt. Futagami, often terrorized the residents of Araragi Village. The climax of his reign of terror came when he kidnapped Princess Unome, intending to forcibly marry her. Mikeiri no Mikoto, upon his return to Takachiho, saw a haunting reflection of Princess Unome in a pond by the Gokase River. This vision revealed Kihachi's sinister intentions. In response, Mikeiri rallied a group of forty-four vassals to storm Kihachi's cave and rescue the princess.<ref name="mw-2" /><ref name="the-official-miyazaki-prefecture-travel-guide-2023a" />
Despite the initial victory, Kihachi was not easily subdued. After his defeat, he resurrected the very next day, burning with rage. In their subsequent encounter, Mikeiri took drastic measures by severing Kihachi into three distinct sections: head, torso, and limbs. Each segment was buried separately to ensure Kihachi's permanent incapacitation. Following this victory, Mikeiri and Princess Unome married, eventually having eight children. The legend asserts that their descendants later ruled over the region of Takachiho.<ref name="mw-2" />
== Modern worship == Today, physical reminders of this legend are scattered throughout {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|WD=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho}}. Among them are the Nanatsuga Pond, where legend says Mikeiri first encountered Princess Unome's reflection, and the Chikara Ishi, a massive boulder believed to have been thrown by Kihachi during their initial battle. Distinct markers indicate the burial spots of Kihachi's divided body: his head is near the Solest Takachiho Hotel, the torso is proximate to Ryokan Shinsen, and his limbs are found near the ruins of Awaji Castle, adjacent to Takachiho High School.<ref name="mw-2" />
In modern {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|WD=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho}}, local folklore says that that Kihachi's vengeful spirit occasionally stirs, casting early frosts that harm the crops. To counteract this, the [[Takachiho Shrine]] hosts an annual event known as the Shishikake Festival. The ceremony involves offering a boar and the chanting of special prayers by Shinto priests to appease Kihachi's spirit. Additionally, Kagura, a traditional Shinto dance, is showcased during the festival, typically taking place on the third day of the twelfth lunar month, often coinciding with January.<ref name="mw-2" /> ==Family tree== {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== See also ==
* [[Nihon Shoki]] * [[Kagura]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== * W.G. Aston (tr.), ''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to AD 697'' (1972 reprint) * [http://www.louis-chor.ca/Nihon-Shoki-Trilingual-January-2020-1.pdf Selections from the Nihon-Shoki]

In response, Mikeiri rallied a group of forty-four vassals to storm Kihachi's cave and rescue the princess.<ref name="mw-2" /><ref name="the-official-miyazaki-prefecture-travel-guide-2023a" />
Despite the initial victory, Kihachi was not easily subdued. After his defeat, he resurrected the very next day, burning with rage. In their subsequent encounter, Mikeiri took drastic measures by severing Kihachi into three distinct sections: head, torso, and limbs. Each segment was buried separately to ensure Kihachi's permanent incapacitation. Following this victory, Mikeiri and Princess Unome married, eventually having eight children. The legend asserts that their descendants later ruled over the region of Takachiho.<ref name="mw-2" />
== Modern worship == Today, physical reminders of this legend are scattered throughout {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|WD=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho}}. Among them are the Nanatsuga Pond, where legend says Mikeiri first encountered Princess Unome's reflection, and the Chikara Ishi, a massive boulder believed to have been thrown by Kihachi during their initial battle. Distinct markers indicate the burial spots of Kihachi's divided body: his head is near the Solest Takachiho Hotel, the torso is proximate to Ryokan Shinsen, and his limbs are found near the ruins of Awaji Castle, adjacent to Takachiho High School.<ref name="mw-2" />
In modern {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|WD=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho}}, local folklore says that that Kihachi's vengeful spirit occasionally stirs, casting early frosts that harm the crops. To counteract this, the [[Takachiho Shrine]] hosts an annual event known as the Shishikake Festival. The ceremony involves offering a boar and the chanting of special prayers by Shinto priests to appease Kihachi's spirit. Additionally, Kagura, a traditional Shinto dance, is showcased during the festival, typically taking place on the third day of the twelfth lunar month, often coinciding with January.<ref name="mw-2" /> ==Family tree== {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== See also ==

{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | father = [[Ugayafukiaezu]] | mother = [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] | spouse = Princess Unome | siblings = Emperor Jimmu }} '''Mikeiri no Mikoto''' ({{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|WD=Q5287|lt=Japanese}}: 三毛入野命, also known as Mikenu, d. 663 BC according to traditional dating) is a Japanese deity. He was the older brother of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}.<ref name="mw-2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001560306.pdf|title=Legend Mikeiri no Mikoto and Kihachi}}</ref> While playing a minor role in the [[Nihon Shoki]],<ref name="nihongi-1972a">''Nihongi'' (W.G. Aston tr., Tuttle 1972 reprint), p. 114</ref><ref name="mw-2" /> he has a prominent local tradition at [[Takachiho Shrine]]<ref name="mw-2" /> of which he is the main deity.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Click the english button to switch the page language to english since that is the page I am citing, not the Japanese version |url=https://www.miyazaki-archive.jp/d-museum/mch/kagura/nokatano-movie.html |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.miyazaki-archive.jp}}</ref> There is an annual festival for him called Shishikake Festival.<ref name="mw-2" />
== Myths ==
=== Nihon Shoki === According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], Mikeiri no Mikoto, [[Ugayafukiaezu]]'s third son and the older brother of Japan's first Emperor Jimmu, assisted his brother the Emperor in government affairs. All his three brothers, including Mikeiri, supported his decision to embark on the military campaign to eastern Japan.<ref>''Nihongi'' (W.G. Aston tr., Tuttle 1972 reprint), p. 111</ref><ref>http://www.louis-chor.ca/Nihon-Shoki-Trilingual-January-2020-1.pdf Selections from Nihon-Shoki, ch. 3</ref> Later during the military campaign, Mikeiri disappeared at sea<ref name="nihongi-1972a" /><ref name="mw-2" /> alongside {{ill|Inahi no Mikoto|en|Inahi no Mikoto|WD=Q11420805|lt=Inahi no Mikoto}} and as a result, Jimmu was left the sole leader and became Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamuyamatoiwarebiko |url=https://archive.today/20230930215114/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9379 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>
=== Takachiho myth === However, {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|WD=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho}} legend offers a different account, suggesting he returned to {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|WD=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho}} to govern it.<ref name="the-official-miyazaki-prefecture-travel-guide-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Main Sanctuary, Takachiho Shrine |url=https://visitmiyazaki.com/mythology/main-sanctuary/ |access-date=2023-09-30 |website=The Official Miyazaki Prefecture Travel Guide |language=en-US}}</ref> The legend further narrates that a formidable kami deity named Kihachi, residing in a cave on Mt. Futagami, often terrorized the residents of Araragi Village. The climax of his reign of terror came when he kidnapped Princess Unome, intending to forcibly marry her. Mikeiri no Mikoto, upon his return to Takachiho, saw a haunting reflection of Princess Unome in a pond by the Gokase River. This vision revealed Kihachi's sinister intentions. In response, Mikeiri rallied a group of forty-four vassals to storm Kihachi's cave and rescue the princess.<ref name="mw-2" /><ref name="the-official-miyazaki-prefecture-travel-guide-2023a" />
Despite the initial victory, Kihachi was not easily subdued. After his defeat, he resurrected the very next day, burning with rage. In their subsequent encounter, Mikeiri took drastic measures by severing Kihachi into three distinct sections: head, torso, and limbs. Each segment was buried separately to ensure Kihachi's permanent incapacitation. Following this victory, Mikeiri and Princess Unome married, eventually having eight children. The legend asserts that their descendants later ruled over the region of Takachiho.<ref name="mw-2" />
== Modern worship == Today, physical reminders of this legend are scattered throughout {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|WD=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho}}. Among them are the Nanatsuga Pond, where legend says Mikeiri first encountered Princess Unome's reflection, and the Chikara Ishi, a massive boulder believed to have been thrown by Kihachi during their initial battle. Distinct markers indicate the burial spots of Kihachi's divided body: his head is near the Solest Takachiho Hotel, the torso is proximate to Ryokan Shinsen, and his limbs are found near the ruins of Awaji Castle, adjacent to Takachiho High School.<ref name="mw-2" />
In modern {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|WD=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho}}, local folklore says that that Kihachi's vengeful spirit occasionally stirs, casting early frosts that harm the crops. To counteract this, the [[Takachiho Shrine]] hosts an annual event known as the Shishikake Festival. The ceremony involves offering a boar and the chanting of special prayers by Shinto priests to appease Kihachi's spirit. Additionally, Kagura, a traditional Shinto dance, is showcased during the festival, typically taking place on the third day of the twelfth lunar month, often coinciding with January.<ref name="mw-2" /> ==Family tree== {{Three generations of Hyuga}}

{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | father = [[Ugayafukiaezu]] | mother = [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] | spouse = Princess Unome | siblings = Emperor Jimmu }} '''Mikeiri no Mikoto''' ({{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|WD=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}}: 三毛入野命, also known as Mikenu, d. 663 BC according to traditional dating) is a Japanese deity. He was the older brother of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}.<ref name="mw-2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001560306.pdf|title=Legend Mikeiri no Mikoto and Kihachi}}</ref> While playing a minor role in the [[Nihon Shoki]],<ref name="nihongi-1972a">''Nihongi'' (W.G. Aston tr., Tuttle 1972 reprint), p. 114</ref><ref name="mw-2" /> he has a prominent local tradition at [[Takachiho Shrine]]<ref name="mw-2" /> of which he is the main deity.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Click the english button to switch the page language to english since that is the page I am citing, not the Japanese version |url=https://www.miyazaki-archive.jp/d-museum/mch/kagura/nokatano-movie.html |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.miyazaki-archive.jp}}</ref> There is an annual festival for him called Shishikake Festival.<ref name="mw-2" />
== Myths ==
=== Nihon Shoki === According to the [[Nihon Shoki]], Mikeiri no Mikoto, [[Ugayafukiaezu]]'s third son and the older brother of Japan's first Emperor Jimmu, assisted his brother the Emperor in government affairs. All his three brothers, including Mikeiri, supported his decision to embark on the military campaign to eastern Japan.<ref>''Nihongi'' (W.G. Aston tr., Tuttle 1972 reprint), p. 111</ref><ref>http://www.louis-chor.ca/Nihon-Shoki-Trilingual-January-2020-1.pdf Selections from Nihon-Shoki, ch. 3</ref> Later during the military campaign, Mikeiri disappeared at sea<ref name="nihongi-1972a" /><ref name="mw-2" /> alongside {{ill|Inahi no Mikoto|en|Inahi no Mikoto|WD=Q11420805|lt=Inahi no Mikoto|1=Inahi no Mikoto}} and as a result, Jimmu was left the sole leader and became Emperor.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamuyamatoiwarebiko |url=https://archive.today/20230930215114/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9379 |website=[[Kokugakuin University]] Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>
=== Takachiho myth === However, {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|WD=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho|1=Takachiho, Miyazaki}} legend offers a different account, suggesting he returned to {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|WD=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho|1=Takachiho, Miyazaki}} to govern it.<ref name="the-official-miyazaki-prefecture-travel-guide-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Main Sanctuary, Takachiho Shrine |url=https://visitmiyazaki.com/mythology/main-sanctuary/ |access-date=2023-09-30 |website=The Official Miyazaki Prefecture Travel Guide |language=en-US}}</ref> The legend further narrates that a formidable kami deity named Kihachi, residing in a cave on Mt. Futagami, often terrorized the residents of Araragi Village. The climax of his reign of terror came when he kidnapped Princess Unome, intending to forcibly marry her. Mikeiri no Mikoto, upon his return to Takachiho, saw a haunting reflection of Princess Unome in a pond by the Gokase River. This vision revealed Kihachi's sinister intentions. In response, Mikeiri rallied a group of forty-four vassals to storm Kihachi's cave and rescue the princess.<ref name="mw-2" /><ref name="the-official-miyazaki-prefecture-travel-guide-2023a" />
Despite the initial victory, Kihachi was not easily subdued. After his defeat, he resurrected the very next day, burning with rage. In their subsequent encounter, Mikeiri took drastic measures by severing Kihachi into three distinct sections: head, torso, and limbs. Each segment was buried separately to ensure Kihachi's permanent incapacitation. Following this victory, Mikeiri and Princess Unome married, eventually having eight children. The legend asserts that their descendants later ruled over the region of Takachiho.<ref name="mw-2" />
== Modern worship == Today, physical reminders of this legend are scattered throughout {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|WD=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho|1=Takachiho, Miyazaki}}. Among them are the Nanatsuga Pond, where legend says Mikeiri first encountered Princess Unome's reflection, and the Chikara Ishi, a massive boulder believed to have been thrown by Kihachi during their initial battle. Distinct markers indicate the burial spots of Kihachi's divided body: his head is near the Solest Takachiho Hotel, the torso is proximate to Ryokan Shinsen, and his limbs are found near the ruins of Awaji Castle, adjacent to Takachiho High School.<ref name="mw-2" />
In modern {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|WD=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho|1=Takachiho, Miyazaki}}, local folklore says that that Kihachi's vengeful spirit occasionally stirs, casting early frosts that harm the crops. To counteract this, the [[Takachiho Shrine]] hosts an annual event known as the Shishikake Festival. The ceremony involves offering a boar and the chanting of special prayers by Shinto priests to appease Kihachi's spirit. Additionally, Kagura, a traditional Shinto dance, is showcased during the festival, typically taking place on the third day of the twelfth lunar month, often coinciding with January.<ref name="mw-2" /> ==Family tree== {{Three generations of Hyuga}}
== See also ==
* [[Nihon Shoki]] * [[Kagura]]
